Organized labor as represented
by the Central Labor union is OPP
posed to any contract being made
by the city with the Washington
Water Power Co. for lights for
streets and public buildings for a
period of more than l year.


The central body has repeatedly
gone on record as favoring NUM-
cipal lighting plant, and last night
that position was reaffirmed in the
appointment of special committee
to protest against entering into any
contract with the local monopoly
extending over l year.


Working men generally believe
that the w. w. P. Co. charges for
arc lights to the city are fairly
reasonable, but are certain that
the charges for residences and
small manufacturing plants are eX-
orbitant. 1t Is pointed out that in
some cities the lighting companies
serve the city gratis wit ha large
number of street lights in consider
alton for their franchises, which
naturally tends to discount the
local power monopoly's contention
that it is particularly generous
with this city.


What Is desired Is reasonable
rate for lights for homes and small
manufacturing plants, and the city
should secure these conditions for
citizens.





Since the installation of NUM-
cipal lighting plant in Seattle the
cost to consumers has been re-
duced more than W per cent. The
